era_context: The interwar period is in deep crisis. The Great Depression has devastated economies worldwide, leading to mass unemployment, social disorder, and political instability. Nazi Germany is on the verge of power, with the Nazi Party winning a plurality in elections. The Soviet Union is pursuing rapid industrialisation and forced collectivisation. Japan is pursuing aggressive expansion in Asia. The League of Nations is proving ineffectual in restraining aggressor states. Democratic institutions are under severe strain in many countries.

events.title: Franklin D. Roosevelt elected President of the United States
events.description: On 8 November 1932, Franklin D. Roosevelt was elected President of the United States, defeating incumbent President Herbert Hoover by a landslide. Roosevelt won 472 electoral votes to Hoover's 59, and received 57.4% of the popular vote. The election was a referendum on Hoover's handling of the Great Depression, which had devastated the American economy and left millions unemployed. Roosevelt's campaign promised a 'New Deal' for the American people, with active government intervention to address the economic crisis. The election marked a fundamental shift in American politics, as voters rejected Hoover's laissez-faire approach and embraced Roosevelt's vision of an activist federal government. Roosevelt's victory also signalled the beginning of a political realignment in American politics, with the Democratic Party becoming the party of active government intervention and the working class.

sources.name: U.S. election records and contemporary newspaper accounts
Kennedy, David M., Freedom from Fear: The American People in Depression and War, 1929–1945 (1999)

events.certainty_note: The date, results, and significance of the 1932 election are extensively documented in election records and contemporary sources.
cross_references: Follows from 1929 (Stock market crash and beginning of Great Depression)

events.title: Nazi Party wins plurality in German elections
events.description: In two elections held in 1932 (31 July and 6 November), the Nazi Party (NSDAP) won a plurality of votes, establishing itself as the largest party in the German Reichstag. In the July election, the Nazis won 37.3% of the vote and 230 seats, making them the largest party. In the November election, the Nazis' share declined slightly to 33.1% and 196 seats, but they remained the largest party. The Nazi electoral success was driven by economic desperation caused by the Great Depression, resentment of the Treaty of Versailles, and fear of communism. Although the Nazis won a plurality, they did not win a majority, and Hitler was not immediately appointed Chancellor. However, the Nazi electoral success demonstrated the party's broad appeal and set the stage for Hitler's appointment as Chancellor in January 1933. The 1932 elections marked a critical turning point in German history, as they demonstrated the vulnerability of the Weimar Republic to extremist parties.

sources.name: German election records and Reichstag documents
Contemporary newspaper accounts from German and international sources
Shirer, William L., The Rise and Fall of the Third Reich (1960)

events.certainty_note: The dates, results, and significance of the 1932 elections are extensively documented in German election records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Bonus Army march on Washington
events.description: In the summer of 1932, approximately 15,000–20,000 World War I veterans, known as the Bonus Army, marched on Washington, D.C., to demand immediate payment of a bonus promised to them by Congress. The bonus was not scheduled to be paid until 1945, but the veterans, many of whom were unemployed and destitute due to the Great Depression, demanded immediate payment. President Hoover, fearing the march would turn into a communist-inspired insurrection, ordered the U.S. Army to disperse the veterans. On 28–29 July 1932, General Douglas MacArthur, with assistance from Major Dwight D. Eisenhower and Major George S. Patton, led troops and tanks to drive the veterans from their camps, resulting in injuries and deaths. The violent dispersal of the Bonus Army was widely condemned and damaged Hoover's reputation, contributing to his defeat in the November 1932 election. The incident demonstrated the desperation of the American people during the Great Depression and the government's harsh response to social unrest.

sources.name: U.S. government records and military reports
Contemporary newspaper accounts and eyewitness reports
Kennedy, David M., Freedom from Fear: The American People in Depression and War, 1929–1945 (1999)

events.certainty_note: The date, sequence of events, and key figures are extensively documented in government records and contemporary accounts.
cross_references: Part of broader social unrest during Great Depression (1929–1939)
Led to 1933-004 (Roosevelt's inauguration and New Deal)

events.title: Lindbergh kidnapping trial and conviction
events.description: In 1932, the trial of Bruno Richard Hauptmann for the kidnapping and murder of Charles Lindbergh Jr., the infant son of famous aviator Charles Lindbergh, captivated American public attention. The kidnapping had occurred on 1 March 1932, and the child's body was discovered on 12 May 1932. Hauptmann was arrested in September 1932 and tried in 1935 (the trial began in January 1935, not 1932, but the kidnapping and initial investigation occurred in 1932). Hauptmann was convicted and executed in 1936. The Lindbergh kidnapping case became one of the most famous criminal cases in American history, and it led to the passage of the 'Lindbergh Law' (the Federal Kidnapping Act of 1932), which made kidnapping a federal crime. The case reflected broader anxieties about crime, social disorder, and the vulnerability of even the wealthy and famous during the Great Depression.

sources.name: Police records and court documents
Contemporary newspaper accounts from American sources
Fisher, Jim, The Lindbergh Case (1987)

events.certainty_note: The kidnapping date and basic facts are well-documented, though the trial occurred in 1935, not 1932.

events.title: Federal Kidnapping Act (Lindbergh Law) passed
events.description: On 22 June 1932, the U.S. Congress passed the Federal Kidnapping Act, commonly known as the Lindbergh Law, which made kidnapping a federal crime. The law was passed in response to the kidnapping and murder of Charles Lindbergh Jr. in March 1932. The law provided for federal jurisdiction over kidnapping cases and established severe penalties, including the death penalty for kidnappings that resulted in death. The Lindbergh Law represented an expansion of federal criminal jurisdiction and reflected public concern about crime and social disorder during the Great Depression. The law also demonstrated the political power of celebrity and public opinion in shaping legislation.

sources.name: U.S. Congressional records and legislative documents
Contemporary newspaper accounts

events.certainty_note: The date and passage of the Federal Kidnapping Act are extensively documented in Congressional records.

events.title: Stimson Doctrine announced
events.description: On 7 January 1932, U.S. Secretary of State Henry Stimson announced the Stimson Doctrine, which declared that the United States would not recognise any territorial changes brought about by force, in response to Japan's invasion of Manchuria in September 1931. The doctrine was a statement of principle rather than a concrete policy, and it had limited practical effect in restraining Japanese aggression. However, the Stimson Doctrine represented an important assertion of American opposition to Japanese expansionism and set a precedent for American non-recognition of territorial conquests. The doctrine was named after Stimson and became a cornerstone of American foreign policy during the 1930s, though it proved ineffective in preventing Japanese aggression.

sources.name: U.S. State Department records and diplomatic correspondence
Contemporary newspaper accounts and diplomatic cables

events.certainty_note: The date and terms of the Stimson Doctrine are extensively documented in State Department records and contemporary sources.
cross_references: Led to 1933-006 (Japan's withdrawal from League of Nations)

events.title: Chaco War between Bolivia and Paraguay
events.description: The Chaco War (1932–1935) was a conflict between Bolivia and Paraguay over control of the Chaco Boreal region in South America. The war began in June 1932 with skirmishes between Bolivian and Paraguayan forces and escalated into a full-scale conflict. The war was motivated by disputes over territorial boundaries and the discovery of oil in the region. The conflict resulted in approximately 100,000 deaths and was one of the deadliest conflicts in South American history. The war demonstrated the fragility of post-WWI border settlements in South America and the limited effectiveness of the League of Nations in resolving territorial disputes outside Europe. The war also highlighted the role of oil and natural resources in driving international conflicts.

sources.name: Bolivian and Paraguayan government records and military documents
League of Nations records and mediation documents
Contemporary newspaper accounts from South American and international sources

events.certainty_note: The basic facts of the Chaco War are well-documented, but the exact sequence of military engagements and casualty figures are less thoroughly recorded in available sources.
cross_references: Related to broader pattern of post-WWI border disputes in South America

events.title: Disarmament Conference in Geneva
events.description: In 1932, the World Disarmament Conference was held in Geneva under the auspices of the League of Nations. The conference was intended to negotiate reductions in military armaments following World War I, but it was undermined by the Great Depression, rising nationalism, and the unwillingness of major powers to make significant concessions. Germany demanded equality in armaments with other European powers, while France insisted on security guarantees before agreeing to disarmament. The conference ultimately failed to achieve meaningful disarmament agreements, and it demonstrated the weakness of the League of Nations in addressing security issues. The failure of the disarmament conference contributed to the arms race of the 1930s and the eventual outbreak of World War II.

sources.name: League of Nations records and conference documents
Contemporary newspaper accounts and diplomatic cables

events.certainty_note: The dates and basic facts of the Disarmament Conference are well-documented in League of Nations records and contemporary sources.
cross_references: Led to 1933-002 (Germany's withdrawal from disarmament conference)

events.title: Éamon de Valera elected President of Irish Free State
events.description: In February 1932, Éamon de Valera and his Fianna Fáil party won the Irish general election, and de Valera became President of the Executive Council of the Irish Free State (equivalent to Prime Minister). De Valera's election marked a significant shift in Irish politics, as Fianna Fáil had previously been excluded from power. De Valera pursued policies of economic self-sufficiency and cultural nationalism, and he worked to reduce Irish dependence on Britain. De Valera's government also pursued a policy of non-alignment in international affairs, refusing to join the League of Nations until 1955. De Valera's presidency marked the beginning of a new phase in Irish independence, as he worked to consolidate Irish sovereignty and reduce British influence.
key_figures: Éamon de Valera (President of the Executive Council, Irish Free State)

sources.name: Irish government records and election documents
Contemporary newspaper accounts from Irish and British sources

events.certainty_note: The date and results of the 1932 Irish election are extensively documented in government records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Manchukuo established as Japanese puppet state
events.description: On 18 February 1932, Japan formally established Manchukuo as an independent state, following its invasion of Manchuria in September 1931. Manchukuo was nominally independent but was in fact a Japanese puppet state, with the last Qing Emperor Puyi installed as a figurehead ruler. The establishment of Manchukuo was a violation of the League of Nations Covenant and the Kellogg-Briand Pact, and it was not recognised by the League of Nations or most countries. The creation of Manchukuo represented a major step in Japanese imperial expansion and demonstrated Japan's willingness to defy international law and the League of Nations. Manchukuo remained under Japanese control until 1945, when it was conquered by Soviet forces.
key_figures: Puyi (Last Qing Emperor, puppet ruler of Manchukuo)

sources.name: Japanese government records and military documents
League of Nations records and contemporary diplomatic cables
Contemporary newspaper accounts from Japanese and international sources

events.certainty_note: The date and basic facts of Manchukuo's establishment are extensively documented in Japanese government records and contemporary sources.
cross_references: Led to 1933-006 (Japan's withdrawal from League of Nations)

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of the Bonus Army march as a purely spontaneous veterans' movement is complicated by evidence of communist and other radical groups' involvement in organising the march, though the veterans' grievances were genuine. The Stimson Doctrine is sometimes portrayed as an effective assertion of American opposition to Japanese aggression, but it had limited practical effect and did not prevent Japanese expansion. The Chaco War is sometimes overlooked in historical accounts focused on European events, but it was a significant conflict with substantial casualties. The Disarmament Conference is sometimes portrayed as a complete failure, but some historians argue that it achieved limited agreements and demonstrated the complexity of disarmament negotiations.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1932 is rich and well-documented, particularly for Western events. However, the record is heavily weighted toward Western sources and perspectives; coverage of events in Asia, Africa, and South America is often sparse or filtered through Western diplomatic reports. The 1932 U.S. election is extensively documented in American sources, but the perspectives of those harmed by the Great Depression are sometimes marginalised. The Nazi electoral success in 1932 is well-documented in German records and contemporary accounts, but the perspectives of those who opposed the Nazis are often underrepresented. The Chaco War is documented in Bolivian and Paraguayan sources, but these sources are often inaccessible to English-language historians. The record is also shaped by Cold War historiography, which emphasised the threat of fascism and communism while sometimes downplaying other significant developments.

graph_edges.note: Roosevelt's election in 1932 led to his inauguration and New Deal in 1933
Nazi electoral success in 1932 led to Hitler's appointment as Chancellor in 1933
Bonus Army march damaged Hoover's reputation, contributing to Roosevelt's election
Lindbergh kidnapping led to passage of Federal Kidnapping Act
Stimson Doctrine in 1932 preceded Japan's withdrawal from League of Nations in 1933
Chaco War began in 1932, continued until 1935
Disarmament Conference failure in 1932 preceded Germany's withdrawal from disarmament in 1933
Manchukuo establishment in 1932 preceded Japan's withdrawal from League of Nations in 1933
